draft.introduction: A polemical psalm mocking the futility of idols while exalting the God of Israel. The contrast between dead idols and the living God became a standard apologetic motif in the early Church.

translation.text: Not to us, O Lord, not to us, but to your name give glory, for the sake of your steadfast love and faithfulness. Why should the nations say, “Where is their God?” Their idols are silver and gold, the work of human hands. They have mouths but do not speak; eyes but do not see.
translation.original_text: μὴ ἡμῖν κύριε μὴ ἡμῖν ἀλλ᾽ ἢ τῷ ὀνόματί σου δὸς δόξαν

translator_notes.note: The idol polemic—“those who make them become like them”—was cited by Athanasius to argue that worship transforms the worshipper into the likeness of what is worshipped, a principle central to theosis theology.
